resumeData.title: AI Engineer & Full-Stack Developer
resumeData.subtitle: AI-Driven Solutions & Data Strategy
resumeData.summary: Hands-on developer specializing in data engineering, machine learning, and web development. Experience building high-frequency data aggregators for digital asset markets and robust data architectures for HealthTech platforms. Proficient across the full data lifecycle: scraping, ETL, deep learning model training (LSTM, GANs, YOLOv8), and deployment with Docker and FastAPI. Focused on clean code, automation, and interfaces that make complex data accessible.

engagements.client: Outlier AI, AI training and model evaluation platform
engagements.project: Supported AI model training by evaluating, annotating, and improving model responses across reasoning, factuality, and instruction-following tasks.
achievements: Evaluated AI responses for accuracy, clarity, safety, and instruction-following
Compared model outputs and identified hallucinations, reasoning errors, and inconsistencies
Provided structured annotations and feedback to improve LLM training data quality
Rewrote and refined responses based on project guidelines

engagements.project: Architected the complete data infrastructure for a nutritional tracking application (mobile and web).
achievements: Contributed to the development of a computer vision model for food segmentation and calorie estimation
Designed and deployed the relational database (SQL) schema from scratch, ensuring 3NF normalization for user logs, nutritional tables, and biometric data
Built ETL pipelines to scrape, clean, and ingest nutritional datasets
Established data cleaning standards to support the calorie recommendation engine

education.degree: MBA, Data Science, Artificial Intelligence & Analytics

education.institution: Federal Institute of Rio Grande do Sul (IFRS)

projects.title: DocMind: Intelligent Document Assistant
projects.description: Developed a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system allowing users to query PDF documents using natural language.

projects.title: Industrial Anomaly Detection: Computer Vision & IoT
projects.description: Built a "Black Piano" anomaly detection system for semiconductor manufacturing (SECOM) and a defect detection model for industrial bottling (MVTec AD).

projects.title: FluxForecast: Industrial Predictive Modeling
projects.description: Created a time-series forecasting system to predict liquid flow in pipeline systems, visualizing output via a real-time dashboard.

projects.title: Advanced Computer Vision Suites
projects.description: Deployed high-precision vision models including a License Plate Recognition system (99.69% precision) and real-time emotion analysis.

hero.label: Mentoring
hero.title: Python & Data
hero.subtitle: Hands-on programming exercises with a data focus. Enrollment required.
hero.notice: Lessons are available only to enrolled students. Get in touch to join.

curriculum.label: Program
curriculum.title: Course modules
curriculum.hint: Expand a module to see lessons, labs, or the final project.

mentoria.comingSoonBody: This module will open soon for enrolled students.

lessons.title: Variables and print()
lessons.description: Practice variables, values, basic types, and print() in Python. Learn how to store information, display results, and solve small problems with simple data.

lessons.title: User input and type conversion
lessons.description: Practice input() and conversions with int() and float(). Understand that typed values arrive as text and must be converted for numeric work.

lessons.title: Operators and simple calculations
lessons.description: Practice math operators in Python: add, subtract, multiply, divide, power, and percentages. Apply them to everyday data scenarios such as sales, payroll, and costs.

lessons.title: Strings for data work
lessons.description: Practice basic text operations: strip(), lower(), upper(), title(), split(), and replace(). These skills matter when cleaning and standardizing real-world text data.

lessons.title: Conditionals: if, elif, and else
lessons.description: Practice decision rules with if, elif, and else to classify values, validate inputs, and branch logic based on the data you receive.

lessons.title: Lists and tuples in Python
lessons.description: Practice lists and tuples: store multiple values, index elements, compare mutable vs immutable collections, and use len(), sum(), max(), and min().

lessons.title: Loops with for and while
lessons.description: Practice for and while loops: iterate sequences, repeat actions, build accumulators, count values, and distinguish iteration from condition-driven repetition.

lessons.title: Dictionaries and lists of dictionaries
lessons.description: Practice structured data with dicts and lists of dicts: access by key, update records, add fields, and navigate collections of objects.

lessons.title: Functions in Python
lessons.description: Practice def, parameters, and return. Build reusable blocks, separate print() from return, use optional parameters, and try simple lambda expressions.

finalProject.title: Order Analysis with Basic Python
finalProject.description: End-of-module capstone. A simple e-commerce order report using pure Python — lists, dictionaries, functions, and terminal output. Available as a portfolio mini-project.

lessons.title: Pandas data exploration and cleaning
lessons.description: Practice loading CSV files, inspecting DataFrames, identifying missing values, converting data types, handling messy values, and creating a cleaned dataset for analysis.

lessons.title: Exploratory analysis and Matplotlib
lessons.description: Practice grouping, filtering, aggregating, and visualizing business questions with Pandas and Matplotlib using a cleaned sales dataset.

lessons.title: Customer churn EDA and preparation
lessons.description: Analyze a customer churn dataset, inspect the target variable, investigate numerical and categorical features, clean TotalCharges, and prepare the data for modeling.

lessons.title: First classification model with Scikit-learn
lessons.description: Train a first churn classification model, split train/test data, encode categorical variables, evaluate predictions, inspect feature importance, and save the model with joblib.

colabGuide.title: How to use the Colab labs
steps: Open the notebook in Google Colab.
Go to File > Save a copy in Drive.
Read the comments and complete the exercises.
colabGuide.notice: No local installation is required. The notebooks run directly in the browser through Google Colab.

cta.title: Want to join the mentoring program?
cta.body: These exercises are part of a private Python & Data course. To access Runestone lessons and the mentoring group, get in touch.

hero.label: Mentoring exercise
hero.title: Order Analysis with Basic Python
hero.subtitle: From Runestone to your computer: build a real Python mini-project with VS Code, a virtual environment, terminal, and GitHub.
hero.description: This guide walks you through creating the project folder, opening VS Code, creating and activating a venv, downloading the .py file, running in the terminal, completing functions, writing a README, and publishing on GitHub.

osSetup.title: Choose your operating system
osSetup.intro: Before starting the exercise, pick your operating system below. Commands differ slightly between Linux/macOS and Windows, especially when activating the virtual environment.
osSetup.venvNote: Even though this project does not use external libraries, creating a virtual environment is good practice. It isolates the project's Python and prepares you for larger projects later.
osSetup.commandsHint: Copy and run the commands below in order in your system's terminal:
commandLegend: code . opens the folder in VS Code.
python -m venv .venv creates the virtual environment.
The activation command depends on your system (see the tab you selected).
osSetup.vscodeNote: If code . does not work, open VS Code manually and use File > Open Folder to select analise-pedidos-python.
osSetup.powershellNote: If PowerShell blocks virtual environment activation, use the Windows CMD tab with .venv\Scripts\activate.bat — or open VS Code and use the integrated terminal set to CMD.
osSetup.downloadBody: After creating the folder and virtual environment, download the file and place it inside analise-pedidos-python:
osSetup.encouragement: If a command does not work the first time, that's okay. Copy the error, ask for help in your group, and try again. Setting up your environment is part of learning.

simulation.title: What does this project simulate?
simulation.intro: The exercise simulates a simple e-commerce order analysis. The structure is designed to feel like real data while using only basic Python.
fields: item list (product, category, unit price, quantity)

concepts.title: Concepts practiced
concepts.subtitle: The exercise combines lessons 01–09 into one analysis workflow.

dataStructure.title: Data structure
explanation: Each item in the list is a dictionary.
Each order has a key called itens.
itens is also a list of dictionaries.
This trains multi-level access, for example:

encouragement.title: You don't need to get everything right the first time
encouragement.body: If you get stuck on a function, that's okay. The goal of this project isn't to finish quickly—it's to practice reading the data, testing ideas, making mistakes, fixing them, and trying again. That process is part of learning to program.

pythonExercise.closingNote: What matters isn't a perfect solution on the first try. What matters is keeping at it until the logic starts to click.

implement.title: What you need to implement
implement.subtitle: Complete the functions marked with TODO in the .py file:

functions.name: calcular_total_pedido(pedido)
functions.desc: Loops through order items and sums totals.

functions.name: calcular_ticket_medio(lista_pedidos)
functions.desc: Total revenue divided by delivered order count.

functions.name: criar_ranking_clientes(lista_pedidos)
functions.desc: Ranks customers by spend on delivered orders.

functions.name: listar_pedidos_acima_de(lista_pedidos, valor_minimo=500)
functions.desc: Lists delivered orders above the minimum total.

functions.name: gerar_relatorio(lista_pedidos)
functions.desc: Builds and prints the full terminal report.

projectSetup.title: How to create the project on your computer
projectSetup.intro: This guide takes you from the course environment to a real Python project on your machine — folder, VS Code, virtual environment, .py file, README, and GitHub.
projectSetup.continuedIntro: Once the project runs on your computer, continue with the steps below to implement, test, and publish.

step7.title: Test one function at a time
step7.body: You do not need to finish everything at once. After implementing a function, test only that one, for example:
step7.note: Run the file, check the result, fix if needed, then move to the next function.

step8.title: Enable the final report
step8.body: When the functions are ready, find the commented line at the end of the file and enable the report call:

step9.title: Creating README.md
step9.body: The README is your project's home page on GitHub. Create README.md in the project folder. You can start from this template:
step9.note: Replace the sample output with the report your script prints in the terminal.

step10.title: Publishing on GitHub
step10.body: In the project folder, initialize Git and make the first commit:
step10.repoHint: Then create a GitHub repository named
step10.githubNote: Follow the instructions GitHub shows to connect the remote and push your project. You do not need to master Git yet — the goal is to publish your solution.

output.title: Expected output example
output.subtitle: Format of the final terminal report (values may vary with the dataset):

why.title: Why this exercise matters
why.body: Before using Pandas, it is important to understand how to manipulate data with pure Python. This project performs manually what libraries will simplify later.
why.quote: Before automating with Pandas, understand the logic behind the data.

cta.title: Ready to practice?
cta.body: Download the file, complete the functions, and publish your solution on GitHub.

pythonExercise.nextStep: The next study step will use Pandas to simplify this kind of analysis — this exercise bridges basic Python and data analysis.
